evidence_quotes.quote: The ad breaks have become much more hostile.
In order to end the ad, you have to press the button.
However when you press it, the advertisement leaves the mobile application and launches the Google Play Store at the page of the advertised game.
That is ridiculous, is it not? Yet this is what this ad service feels like.

paragraphs.text: I want to cover the astonishingly bad yet overlooked issue of mobile game advertisements. Considering the fact that most of the viewers of these ads are children, the ads should legally be expected to be of high quality. For this form, I decided to play some mobile games for 2 evenings and captutre some appalling advertisements - I have attached some examples in a PDF file. Unsubstantiated claims In the first page of the PDF file, you can see a claim that a video game reduces stress, giving a percentage of people who claim so. This statement is unsourced and made up. The second page has a question "How old is your brain?" in grammatically incorrect Polish. This suggests the game can somehow tell you your brain age, which is not true. Predatory ad break mechanism The ad breaks have become much more hostile. In the second page you can also see a button that reads ">> Google Play". This ad break works as follows: you have to watch the first few seconds of the ad, then the button appears. In order to end the ad, you have to press the button. However when you press it, the advertisement leaves the mobile application and launches the Google Play Store at the page of the advertised game. Then you must exit the store and return to the application you were moved from and finally press the close button to return to the app. That is ridiculous, is it not? Yet this is what the ad engine AppLovin Ads does. Imagine if a television ad gave a phone number but then called your phone and picked it up for you. This sounds silly, but this is what this ad service feels like. Kids deal with this sort of ads, so this user-unfriendly practice is especially unethical, manipulative and predatory! In the third page of the file you can see a similar but less egregious example. Instead of moving the user out of the application, the Google Play page is embedded and forcibly popped up in the ad. This behavior from Unity Ads is also reprehensible. Weirdness or inappropriate content The fourth page ad is trying to hook users in by being weird. It reads: "I tried 273 times, but I still can't find all 98 cats.". The fifth page does not show much (as it is not animated), yet it is good to mention: the frightened woman in the ad breathes in an anatomically incorrect way: instead of her chest, her breasts expand and contract. I have no doubt this is intentional sexualisation of the character. Advertisement not showing game content I have seen advertisements that simply do not show the core content of the game, instead showing elements that do not appear in the game or appear in the minority. Examples include Fishdom (ads showing only the eat-smaller-fish-to-get-bigger element which is still not as is in the ad) and Hero Wars (for details see the YouTube video by Cybercell: "Hero Wars Honest Review - Gem or Scam?") Other egregious examples from before I learned about this form (and thus unrecorded) include: gross content like showing a dehydrated fish with a worm poking out of it (Kids saw it!), ads showing IQ/brain age directly increasing or decreasing along with progress in a puzzle, gambling games using likenesses of banks and TV channels without permission, outright false claims (like an ad lying the advertised game has no ads). For other examples, see the "Awful Game Ads on Instagram" YouTube video series by Danny Gonzalez and the YouTube video "The Worst Mobile Game Ads On Instagram" by Jarvis Johnson. I believe this situation can be combated by ad break regulations, for example: do not force the user to see the application store page, include a comprehensive bad ad reporting system accessible from inside the ad break, make it possible to close the ad in 2 taps or less. I also want to mention problems with age rating exploits. Long story short, there is a PEGI 12 game that is erotic in nature, I could not send a complaint through PEGI's website, had to send an e-mail, did not get a reply. The last page of the PDF file contains the e-mail I sent to PEGI.
